title: The Island Grounds Meeting Itself Is the Retort to Misguided Criticism!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. The source scan confirms that the title ends with `பதிலடி`, not the OCR form `பதிலழு`. It also restores the severely corrupted constitutional terms `Democratic Socialism`, `Preamble`, `Socialist`, and `Secular`. These and other unambiguous scan readings are repaired only in the Tamil reading copy below; the canonical Tamil source remains unchanged.
paragraphs: Yesterday, 5 May 2016, the President of the All India Congress Committee, Mrs Sonia Gandhi, and I took part in a grand election campaign meeting at Island Grounds in Chennai ahead of the 2016 Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly election. Looking at that gathering, it appeared, just as I said in my speech, to be a public celebration of victory.

In her speech, Sonia Gandhi referred to the devastating floods that struck Chennai at the end of last year. She pointed out that the AIADMK Government then in office in Tamil Nadu had not come forward to help the affected people. “Where did the AIADMK Government go? Did it come forward to wipe away the people's tears?” she asked. She also explained that development and progress in Tamil Nadu had been brought to a full stop after 2011.

As Sonia Gandhi spoke, my thoughts went back to the 1980 election, when the Congress Party under Mother Indira Gandhi and the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam under my leadership formed an alliance and contested together. Referring to that, I recalled how I welcomed Indira Gandhi when she came to campaign in that election: “Come, daughter of Nehru! Give us a stable government!”

Later, during the 2004 parliamentary general election, Mrs Sonia Gandhi came to Tamil Nadu to campaign. I welcomed and greeted her with the words: “Come, daughter-in-law of Indira! Triumph, noble daughter of India!”

Even before friendships and alliances were formed with other parties for the coming Tamil Nadu general election, the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam and the Congress Party formed and announced their alliance. At the time, certain parties in Tamil Nadu questioned this alliance.
It was Pandit Nehru who introduced into Indian political thought the great ideal of **Democratic Socialism**, or “ஜனநாயக சோஷலிசம்.”

Through the Forty-second Amendment in 1976, Mrs Indira Gandhi added the precious concepts **Socialist** and **Secular**, meaning “சமதர்ம” and “சமயச் சார்பற்ற,” to the Preamble of the Constitution of India.
Are the ideals and noble principles introduced by those great leaders respected under Jayalalithaa's AIADMK Government in Tamil Nadu? Are they practised? The answer is no.

Democracy is a form of government. In it, the people themselves create the government, participate in every function, and are supported at every level in governing themselves. Democracy is government of the people, conducted by the people, for the people.
Socialism, or social equality, is an economic ideal. Under it, all the wealth of society must be capable of being owned collectively by all the people within that society.
It was against this magnificent background of ideals that Mother Indira Gandhi, when amending the Preamble of our Constitution, framed India as a “secular republic” and a “socialist republic.”

Under Jayalalithaa's rule, democracy has been crushed. Legislative democracy has no place here; democratic conventions have no place either. Opposition parties are not permitted in the Tamil Nadu Legislative Assembly to present their differences of opinion or fair criticism of government actions. The Assembly itself has been turned into a durbar hall for singing Jayalalithaa's praises. The Opposition is gagged; anyone who attempts even constructive criticism of the AIADMK is expelled wholesale.

Freedom of expression has been ruined under Jayalalithaa's rule. Freedom of the press is in grave danger. Cases are filed against newspapers; journalists are attacked. Defamation cases are filed against Opposition leaders. Why, a case has even been filed against me.

Is socialism, or social equality, respected under Jayalalithaa's AIADMK Government? No. The noble ideal of socialism has been turned into an object of ridicule by the AIADMK. Its ministers and members have shown their skill only in plunder. Each has allegedly amassed crores through bribery and corruption from public tax revenue and funds meant for public welfare. They have reportedly used thousands of crores gathered through such corruption to acquire bungalows, estates and hotels abroad, and placed money in foreign banks as private savings for their future politics. Yet they have shown no concern for the poor, the vulnerable or the middle classes. They possess not even the slightest thought of social equality. When Jayalalithaa herself has not a trace of socialist thinking, how could her party members possess it?

Thus, under Jayalalithaa's rule, the death knell has been sounded for democracy and socialism has been driven far away. In short, Pandit Nehru's democratic socialism and the socialist conviction affirmed by Mrs Indira Gandhi receive no respect whatsoever under Jayalalithaa's Government.

The Congress and the DMK possess deep faith in secularism and are firmly committed to democracy and socialism. That is why, on the basis of policies and programmes, they have formed alliances and contested together over a long period. Those who, like Jayalalithaa, have no faith in democracy, socialism or secularism; who enacted an anti-conversion law and indirectly humiliated minority communities; and who sent people for *kar seva*, cannot even imagine the principled political relationship represented by the long friendship between the Congress and the DMK.

When I made the request, it was Mrs Indira Gandhi who undertook the socialist measure of nationalising the banks. When I made the request, it was Mrs Sonia Gandhi who took action to declare Tamil a classical language. When I asked, it was the United Progressive Alliance Government that approved the Sethusamudram Project.

The work on the Maduravoyal-Port “Super Express Highway,” intended to protect and improve the historic Chennai Port, began under the UPA Government. Yet merely because these were achievements accomplished jointly by the DMK and Congress, Jayalalithaa has, out of animosity, neglected Classical Tamil and blocked the elevated Maduravoyal-Port road project. Was it not because the Congress and the DMK formed an alliance that Tamil Nadu obtained numerous progressive measures, including the abolition of CENVAT, the establishment of the Maritime University, Nokia's establishment of a factory, and low-interest education loans for students?
Nor is that all. The projects provided to Tamil Nadu during the Kazhagam administration by Mrs Sonia Gandhi and Prime Minister Manmohan Singh were greater than anything the State had received before:

* Rs 56,664 crore for roads, flyovers and port works across Tamil Nadu; * the Chennai Metro Rail Project at a cost of Rs 14,600 crore; * the Sethusamudram Project at a cost of Rs 2,427 crore; * an internationally standard rolling mill at Salem at a cost of Rs 1,553 crore; * the Hogenakkal Integrated Drinking Water Project at a cost of Rs 1,330 crore; * another seawater desalination project for Chennai at a cost of Rs 908 crore; * the facility to make telephone calls anywhere in India for fifty paise; * ninety railway flyovers at a cost of Rs 1,828 crore; * the National Automotive Testing and Research and Development Infrastructure Project at a cost of Rs 470 crore; * the upgrading of the Government Mohan Kumaramangalam Medical College Hospital in Salem into a super-speciality hospital at a cost of Rs 120 crore; * 27 per cent reservation in Union Government educational institutions; * the Central Institute of Classical Tamil in Chennai; * the Indian Institute of Management in Tiruchirappalli; * Asia's first national institute for persons with disabilities near Chennai; * the National Institute of Siddha in Tambaram; * the Central University in Tiruvarur; * repeal of the POTA law; * the National Rural Employment Guarantee programme; and * the hundred-day employment scheme.
Tamil Nadu has achieved great growth through these major projects. I was able to cite only a few of them at yesterday's meeting.
Without understanding these historic events, certain parties in Tamil Nadu criticised the alliance between the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam and the Congress.

Sonia Gandhi's presence and speech at yesterday's Island Grounds public meeting served as the answer to their questions. Yesterday's Island Grounds meeting itself was the fitting retort to their misguided criticism!
With affection,
M.K.
